serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve Qoo bottle or carton contains approximately 250 ml, providing around 110 kcal and 25 g of sugar per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Qoo (any flavour) — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Qoo (any flavour), cr_id D03070031. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Qoo branded fruit drink found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 63 is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry: 'Fanta Orange soft drink' and 'fruit punch' entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 63–68 for sugar-sweetened fruit-flavoured beverages). A midpoint of 63 is used. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (63 × 26.375) / 100 = 16.7. Serving size used: 250 ml = 250 g, carbohydrate = 10.55 g/100 g × 250 g / 100 = 26.375 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (10.1 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation, despite low sodium (5 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Qoo and all sugar-sweetened beverages; replace with plain water, unsweetened green tea, or sparkling water, as regular SSB consumption is independently associated with elevated blood pressure regardless of dietary sodium intake (AHA 2018).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 10.1 g sugar/100 g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial blood glucose spikes and contributing to insulin resistance with habitual consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid Qoo entirely, as a 250 ml serving delivers approximately 25 g of rapidly absorbed sugar with no fibre buffer, producing a significant glycaemic load (GL ~16.7) and acute hyperglycaemia risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, increased visceral adiposity, and higher cardiovascular event risk independent of total caloric intake.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should eliminate Qoo and other SSBs from their diet, as habitual SSB intake is linked to dyslipidaemia, metabolic syndrome, and a 20% higher risk of coronary heart disease per daily serving (Malik et al., Circulation 2010).

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar content (10.1 g/100 g) with zero anti-inflammatory nutrients; sugar-sweetened beverages promote pro-inflammatory cytokine production and worsen systemic inflammation relevant to RA disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Qoo, as sugar-sweetened beverages promote systemic inflammation through advanced glycation end-products and pro-inflammatory mediators, with no offsetting anti-inflammatory nutrients present.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Qoo is a commercially manufactured, fruit-flavoured sugar-sweetened soft drink produced by The Coca-Cola Company, widely sold in Singapore and across Asia in a variety of fruit flavours including grape, orange, and apple. It is an ultra-processed, ready-to-drink beverage formulated from water, sugar, fruit juice concentrate, food acids, natural and artificial flavourings, and food colouring. The drink has a sweet, fruity taste with a light, watery texture and no carbonation. Per 100 ml, Qoo provides 44 kcal, 10.55 g of carbohydrate (of which 10.1 g is sugar), negligible protein and fat, and 5 mg of sodium. A standard 250 ml serving delivers approximately 110 kcal and 25 g of sugar. Qoo is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and carries significant clinical concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The high sugar content with zero dietary fibre means liquid glucose is absorbed rapidly, producing acute postprandial blood glucose spikes and a glycaemic load of approximately 16.7 per serving. Regular SSB consumption is independently associated with elevated blood pressure, dyslipidaemia, insulin resistance, and increased cardiovascular event risk. Qoo is not recommended as part of a health-promoting diet and should be replaced with plain water, unsweetened tea, or low-sugar alternatives.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in Qoo?
Is Qoo a juice or soft drink?
Can I drink Qoo if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D03070031) with confidence 0.95; this is an approved primary source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using comparable SSB entries, clearly documented.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero by formulation — Qoo is a sugar-water-based fruit-flavoured beverage containing no protein-bearing ingredients. fat_g is confirmed zero by formulation — the product contains no fat-bearing ingredients. saturated_fat_g and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero for the same reason. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — no fibre-contributing ingredients are present. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 per HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this commercial beverage are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for Qoo (any flavour) as of 2026. GI of 63 is an analogous estimate based on comparable sugar-sweetened fruit-flavoured beverages (Fanta Orange, fruit punch) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Qoo was found in any approved source. Glycemic load per 250 ml serving = (63 × 26.375) / 100 = 16.7. This record covers all flavour variants generically as per the HPB source entry.
